## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Next: jjx-drop-state-legibility (₢BDAAP) [rough]

Docket:
  ## Character
  Spook — small JJK legibility tweak; judgment on where the state-confirmation belongs.
  
  ## Memory (260603)
  Dropping ₢BXAAK, `jjx_drop` returned only a bare commit hash. The drop is additive — it pushes an `abandoned`-state tack onto the pace and never purges it — so `jjx_coronets` still lists the pace and `jjx_brief` still returns its docket verbatim, both presenting an abandoned pace indistinguishably from a live one. Verifying with those two reads, an agent misread a successful drop as a failure and contradicted itself across turns; only `jjx_show`'s State column revealed `abandoned`. `jjx_validate` passed throughout — the command did exactly its job; only its effect was illegible.
  
  ## Goal
  Make a successful drop confirmable from the commands used to perform and verify it, so a tombstoned pace can't be mistaken for a live one.
  
  ## Recommendation
  - Primary — `jjx_drop` should echo the prior→new state in its return (e.g. `₢BXAAK: rough → abandoned`) rather than a bare commit hash; a state mutation should confirm its own effect. Optional sibling extension (same prior→new echo, NOT required for done): `jjx_close` (rough→complete), `jjx_alter` (racing↔stabled), `jjx_archive` (heat→archived).
  - Secondary — quick reads that surface abandoned paces should distinguish them: `jjx_brief` leads with an `[abandoned]` marker; `jjx_coronets` tags abandoned in its default listing.
  
  ## Verify first (260608)
  `jjx_coronets {remaining: true}` ALREADY excludes abandoned (confirmed this session — dropped paces vanish from the remaining list). So the coronets residual is only the DEFAULT (no-`remaining`) call, which still lists abandoned unmarked — tag it there, do NOT re-add a filter. Likewise re-check `jjx_brief`'s current output before assuming it lacks a marker.
  
  ## Done
  - A drop's return names the prior→new state; `jjx_brief` and the default `jjx_coronets` listing visibly mark abandoned paces; confirming a drop no longer requires `jjx_show`.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BD

## Context

Tactical heat for V4-era JJK improvements that are ready to mount — settled designs, mechanical fixes, and concrete spook repairs. Carved out of ₣Am (jjk-v5-notional) on 260507 to surface unblocked work that was hidden in the parking lot.

## Character

Fast tweaks. Bounded scope. No architectural change.

A pace lives here only if its docket is mount-ready — design settled, files identified, exit criteria concrete. If a pace grows hair (uncovered design questions, scope creep, prerequisite hunts), bump it back to ₣Am rather than letting it linger here.

## Inclusion criteria

- Single-file or small-cluster edit
- Spec change with worked-out shape
- Spook fix with clear repair
- Investigation with reproduction already known

## Exclusion criteria

- Open design questions ("which option?")
- Multi-pace dependency chains beyond what's already enrolled
- Anything that would benefit from chat-time design conversation before slating

## Origin

Restrung from ₣Am on 260507 during groom session. Candidates identified as "ready" based on docket maturity:

- AAK — clarify-furlough-verb-in-claude-context (mechanical, single file)
- AAL — capture-session-and-officium-in-git-trailer (settled in ☉260418-1002)
- AAM — size-guard-review-not-split (spook fix, clear shape)
- AAN — jjx-paddock-honor-size-limit-param (spook fix + audit)
- AAH — harden-paddock-encoding (settled; verify ₣Ag prereq at mount)
- AAO — diagnose-localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation (investigation, reproduction known)

## V4 currency

Each pace's docket was scanned for V3-era assumptions before transfer. All references current MCP surface (jjx_open, gazette, officium, current commit-pattern grammar). Prereq verification still required at mount-time for AAH (₣Ag migration completion).

## Related Heats

- ₣Am (jjk-v5-notional) — source parking lot; remaining paces stay there
- ₣Ah (jjk-v4-1-school-breeze-founding) — primary V4 development heat
- ₣An (jjk-v4-2-release-and-legacy-removal) — V4 cleanup heat

# jjezs_pace ₢BDAAP

## Character
Spook — small JJK legibility tweak; judgment on where the state-confirmation belongs.

## Memory (260603)
Dropping ₢BXAAK, `jjx_drop` returned only a bare commit hash. The drop is additive — it pushes an `abandoned`-state tack onto the pace and never purges it — so `jjx_coronets` still lists the pace and `jjx_brief` still returns its docket verbatim, both presenting an abandoned pace indistinguishably from a live one. Verifying with those two reads, an agent misread a successful drop as a failure and contradicted itself across turns; only `jjx_show`'s State column revealed `abandoned`. `jjx_validate` passed throughout — the command did exactly its job; only its effect was illegible.

## Goal
Make a successful drop confirmable from the commands used to perform and verify it, so a tombstoned pace can't be mistaken for a live one.

## Recommendation
- Primary — `jjx_drop` should echo the prior→new state in its return (e.g. `₢BXAAK: rough → abandoned`) rather than a bare commit hash; a state mutation should confirm its own effect. Optional sibling extension (same prior→new echo, NOT required for done): `jjx_close` (rough→complete), `jjx_alter` (racing↔stabled), `jjx_archive` (heat→archived).
- Secondary — quick reads that surface abandoned paces should distinguish them: `jjx_brief` leads with an `[abandoned]` marker; `jjx_coronets` tags abandoned in its default listing.

## Verify first (260608)
`jjx_coronets {remaining: true}` ALREADY excludes abandoned (confirmed this session — dropped paces vanish from the remaining list). So the coronets residual is only the DEFAULT (no-`remaining`) call, which still lists abandoned unmarked — tag it there, do NOT re-add a filter. Likewise re-check `jjx_brief`'s current output before assuming it lacks a marker.

## Done
- A drop's return names the prior→new state; `jjx_brief` and the default `jjx_coronets` listing visibly mark abandoned paces; confirming a drop no longer requires `jjx_show`.

content.text:
**Mounted `₣BD` → `₢BDAAP` (jjx-drop-state-legibility) [rough]**

**Goal:** make a successful `jjx_drop` confirmable from the commands you'd use to perform and verify it — right now a dropped pace tombstones to `abandoned` but still shows up live in `jjx_brief` and default `jjx_coronets`, so only `jjx_show`'s State column reveals it worked.

content.text:
Stopping. I've made **no edits** — only reads — so the tree is clean. Here's the honest assessment against your condition.

## Is this a schema change?

**Split verdict — two of the three planned changes are not; one is.**

**Not a schema change:**
- **Enum-home consolidation** — adding a canonical `jjrg_PaceState::jjrg_as_str()` and pointing the two existing duplicated matches (`zjrsc_pace_state_str`, the inline match in `jjro_ops.rs`) at it. Pure internal Rust. The serde representation (`rename_all="lowercase"`) is untouched; the Gallops JSON storage schema and the `jjrg_PaceState` variants don't move.
- **Primary — `jjx_drop` transition echo.** I can make this *additive*: add a leading `₢BDAAP: rough → abandoned` line and **keep** the existing `committed <hash>` line beneath it. Anything extracting the hash by line still finds it. This changes human/agent-facing confirmation text, not a parsed format.

**Effectively a schema change — the Secondary half:**
- **`jjx_brief` `[abandoned]` marker** — `brief` is documented and implemented as returning the docket text *verbatim* (`jjrgs_run_get_spec` just emits `tack.text`). Prepending a marker line means the return is no longer the raw docket — anything that round-trips brief→redocket or compares against stored text sees drift.
- **`jjx_coronets` `[abandoned]` tag** — `coronets` is specified as a clean *bare-ID-per-line* list ("no silks, no docket"). Appending `  [abandoned]` to a line breaks the "each line is exactly a coronet" contract for any line-wise consumer. That's a wire-format change to a machine-consumed command.

## The bind

The pace's own **`## Done`** requires *all three* — drop echo **and** brief marker **and** coronets tag. So honoring "stop if schema change" means the pace can't reach Done as written: its Secondary half *is* the schema-shaped part.

## What I'd do

Two clean options — your call:

1. **Land the non-schema core now, reslate the rest.** Do the enum-home consolidation + additive drop echo (no format breakage), and reslate the docket to drop or rethink the brief/coronets markers. The drop echo alone substantially fixes the original spook — a drop now confirms its own effect without `jjx_show`.
2. **Treat the whole pace as a deliberate format change** and proceed with all three, accepting the brief/coronets contract shift (possibly with a consumer audit first — I haven't checked whether anything actually parses those two outputs).

I haven't searched for real consumers of the `brief`/`coronets` output yet — say the word and I'll audit before either path. Which way?

lastPrompt: proceed.

content.content:
254	## String Boundary Discipline
255	
256	**Core principle**: This is an internal system. Strings that cross serialization boundaries (JSON, display, CLI) are produced and consumed by the same codebase. There are no external users typing variants.
257	
258	### Single Definition Rule
259	
260	Any string value that appears in serialization or display must have exactly **one** const definition. Both parse and emit reference that const.
261	
262	```rust
263	// ✅ Single definition - const is the source of truth
264	const JJRG_STATE_ROUGH: &str = "rough";
265	
266	impl jjrg_PaceState {
267	    pub fn jjrg_as_str(&self) -> &'static str {
268	        match self {
269	            Self::Rough => JJRG_STATE_ROUGH,
270	            Self::Bridled => JJRG_STATE_BRIDLED,
271	            // ...
272	        }
273	    }
274	
275	    pub fn jjrg_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
276	        match s {
277	            JJRG_STATE_ROUGH => Some(Self::Rough),
278	            JJRG_STATE_BRIDLED => Some(Self::Bridled),
279	            // ...
280	            _ => None,  // Bug upstream, not our problem
281	        }
282	    }
283	}
284	```
285	
286	```rust
287	// ❌ String literal appears twice - immature interface
288	impl jjrg_PaceState {
289	    pub fn jjrg_as_str(&self) -> &'static str {
290	        match self {
291	            Self::Rough => "rough",  // First occurrence
292	        }
293	    }
294	
295	    pub fn jjrg_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
296	        match s {
297	            "rough" => Some(Self::Rough),  // Second occurrence - drift risk
298	        }
299	    }
300	}
301	```
302	
303	### No Friendly Parsing
304	
305	Do not accommodate input variants. If the canonical form is `"rough"`, do not also accept `"Rough"`, `"ROUGH"`, or `"r"`. Flexible parsing is for external APIs with human users — not internal systems.
306	
307	```rust
308	// ❌ Web-brain: accommodating sloppy input
309	"A" | "a" | "APPROACH" | "approach" | "Approach" => Ok(Self::Approach),
310	
311	// ✅ Internal system: exact match only
312	JJRN_MARKER_APPROACH => Ok(Self::Approach),
313	```
314	
315	If input doesn't match the canonical const exactly, that's a bug in the producer — not a variant to handle gracefully.
316	
317	This applies equally to error diagnostics. Do not add "did you mean?" suggestions, fuzzy matching, or edit-distance scoring to error paths. The producer and consumer are the same codebase — a mismatched string is a bug to fix, not a typo to guess at. List the valid values and stop.
318	
319	### Serde Integration
320	
321	Prefer `#[serde(rename = "...")]` or `#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]` over manual string methods when serde handles both serialization and deserialization. The attribute is the single definition.
322	
323	When manual methods are needed (e.g., for display or CLI), define consts that match the serde representation.
324	
325	### Smell Test
326	
327	If `grep '"rough"'` finds the same string literal in multiple places, the interface is immature. Extract a const.
328	
329	## Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings)
330	
331	String Boundary Discipline covers serialized strings. This section extends to **all** repeated values.
332	
333	### Rule
334	
335	Any magic value that appears 2+ times requires a const. This includes numbers, format strings, and computed values.
336	
337	### Derivation Rule
338	
339	If value B depends on value A, always derive B from A at the use site. Never define B as a separate constant.
340	
341	```rust
342	// ✅ Length derived from the const
343	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT: &str = "0000000";
344	let short_arg = format!("--short={}", JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len());
345	
346	fn zjjrg_is_commit_sha(s: &str) -> bool {
347	    s.len() == JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len() && s.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_hexdigit())
348	}
349	
350	// ❌ Magic number exists separately — drift risk
351	const COMMIT_LEN: usize = 7;  // Could diverge from JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT
352	format!("--short=7")          // Hardcoded, not derived
353	```
354	
355	### Identity Rule
356	
357	The const name must identify the specific value, not just its category. A category-only suffix is ambiguous and becomes a terminal exclusivity violation the moment a second value appears in the same category.
358	
359	```rust
360	// ✅ Suffix identifies the specific command
361	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME_GET_SPEC: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
362	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_BIND: &str = "jjx_bind";
363	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND: &str = "jjx_send";
364	
365	// ❌ Suffix names the role, not the identity — deferred terminal exclusivity debt
366	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
367	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_bind";  // Blocks JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND
368	```
369	
370	### Smell Test
371	
372	If `grep '7'` (or any literal) finds the same value used for the same purpose in multiple places, extract a const and derive.
373

1. **There are two parallel type files.** `jjrt_v3_types.rs` defines a *complete duplicate* of `jjrg_PaceState`/`jjrg_HeatStatus`/tack/etc., but nothing imports it except its `pub mod` line in `lib.rs` — it's orphaned dead code. The live types live in `jjrt_types.rs` (re-exported via `jjrg_gallops`). My first edit landed in the dead file; caught it because the live enum uses `#[serde(rename="jjgte_*")]` while the dead one uses `rename_all="lowercase"`. **Not my pace** — flagging it as a likely spook, not acting.

2. **Display label ≠ serde token, deliberately.** The persisted form is `jjgte_rough`; the human label scout/ops emit is `rough`. So `jjrg_as_str` is correctly a *display* home, separate from the serde wire form — that's why I named/commented it as display-only.

## To finish option 1 (when you resume)
- Repoint `jjtsc_scout.rs` (4 tests + import) — unbreaks compile.
- `jjro_ops.rs` inline match → `.jjrg_as_str()`.
- `jjtq_query.rs` tautology → real `jjrg_as_str` test.
- Additive `jjx_drop` echo in `jjrtl_tally.rs` (leading `₢…: rough → abandoned`, keep `committed <hash>`).
- Build + `fast` suite.

Holding here.

message.content: Help me understand if you just did a 'churn', lots of changes that if considered with what you know now, wouldn't have been made?

content.text:
Honest answer: **partly yes.** Let me separate the real churn from the legitimate work, because they're not the same.

## Clear churn — the dead-file round trip
I added consts + `jjrg_as_str` to `jjrt_v3_types.rs`, then reverted all of it when I discovered it was orphaned. Net effect on the tree: **zero**. Pure wasted motion.

Root cause, stated plainly: an early grep surfaced the enum at `jjrt_v3_types.rs:27`, I read that file, and I started editing it *without confirming it was the definition wired into the live re-export path*. The signal I needed — `jjrg_gallops` does `pub use crate::jjrt_types::*` (not `v3_types`) — I only checked **after** editing. For a type that turns out to have a duplicate, the correct first move was to trace the import chain, not to edit the first definition I found. That's a self-inflicted lap.

## Scope expansion — defensible but real
The scout/ops/test refactor (remove `zjrsc_pace_state_str`, repoint call sites and tests) is *surviving, non-zero* work — not churn in the net-zero sense. But it's bigger than this "fast tweak" pace strictly needed. The pace's goal is narrowly **drop legibility**. To echo a state in `jjx_drop` I needed *a* display string — I did not strictly need to relocate the canonical home onto the enum and sweep every duplication.

You did green-light "enum-home consolidation" — but I'm the one who bundled that phrase into "option 1." A more minimal framing was available and I didn't offer it: *extract the display strings to consts, point the existing `zjrsc_pace_state_str` at them, use that from drop* — fixes the magic-string smell you flagged, touches fewer files, and never breaks the build.

## Process slip
I removed the scout helper before fixing its tests, so the tree is sitting **non-compiling**. Even if the consolidation is wanted, the edit order was wrong — I should have kept it green at every step.

## Not churn
The consts + `jjrg_as_str` in the **live** `jjrt_types.rs`, and the planned additive drop echo — that's the actual goal, done correctly (and the magic-string correction you prompted made it RCG-clean). I'd keep that.

## What I'd do differently
1. Trace the re-export chain before editing a type with a possible twin. Would have erased the entire dead-file lap.
2. Offer you the *minimal* version explicitly, not just the consolidating one.
3. Sequence edits to never break compile.

Given you're about to merge and then do the schema change "safely here" — do you want me to **trim back to the minimal version** (keep `zjrsc_pace_state_str`, const-ify it, leave the enum/tests alone, just const + drop echo), or **finish the consolidation** as scoped? I lean minimal now, given the imminent merge and that less surface = safer merge.

content.text:
Change set is exactly the six intended files — and `jjrt_v3_types.rs` (the dead file) is absent, confirming the revert was byte-clean. Net **+42 / −59**: the consolidation removed more than it added.

## Option 1 complete — verified

**What landed (no schema change):**
- **`jjrt_types.rs`** — canonical home: `JJRG_STATE_*` display-label consts + `jjrg_PaceState::jjrg_as_str()` referencing them (RCG single-definition; commented as display-label, distinct from the `jjgte_*` serde wire form).
- **`jjrsc_scout.rs`, `jjro_ops.rs`** — the two duplicated state→string matches now call `.jjrg_as_str()`; the redundant `zjrsc_pace_state_str` helper is gone.
- **`jjtq_query.rs`** — the tautology test became a real all-variant `jjrg_as_str` test against the consts; **`jjtsc_scout.rs`** — the four orphaned helper-tests removed.
- **`jjrtl_tally.rs`** — `jjx_drop` now emits `₢BDAAP: rough → abandoned` then the preserved `committed <hash>` line. Additive; the hash line is untouched, so nothing that reads it breaks.

**Verification:** `vow-b` builds clean under `#![deny(warnings)]` (so the import removals were correct). `vow-t` → **333 JJK tests pass**, including the new `jjtq_pace_state_as_str`; 0 failed.

**Honest caveat:** I did *not* exercise `jjx_drop`'s new stdout live — that would mean abandoning a real pace, and the MCP server is likely still on the pre-rebuild binary anyway. The change is additive and the prior-state capture is straightforward, but the exact runtime line is reasoned-safe, not observed. There's no existing unit harness for `jjrtl_run_drop` (it does real git I/O); adding one is out of scope.

## Two things for you to decide

1. **Don't wrap yet.** The pace's `## Done` also requires the `brief`/`coronets` markers — which *are* the deferred schema change. So this is the non-schema half; ₢BDAAP isn't fully done by its own criteria. Wrapping now would misreport. Better to leave it open for the schema-change session (and possibly reslate the docket to record the split).
2. **Commit?** I left all six files uncommitted for your merge flow — I haven't notched. Say the word and I'll `jjx_record` them against ₢BDAAP with the explicit file list; otherwise they're yours to fold into the merge.

The dead-file observation from earlier (`jjrt_v3_types.rs` is a fully orphaned duplicate of the type set) still stands as a separate spook worth a pace someday — not touching it now.

**Note**: V3 `direction` mapped to pace-level `warrant` in earlier designs. Per cchat-20260317, warrants now live per-beat in the gallops beat table (designed in ₢AhAAQ). Whether pace retains a `warrant` field (possibly repurposed or vestigial for migration) is for ₢AhAAA to determine. Beat table type implementation is a separate concern from this pace.

## Migration transform
In jjdr_load V3 migration path: deserialize as V3 types (jjrt_v3_types), extract tacks[0] values into flat V4 fields. Tack history (tacks[1..]) is discarded — git history preserves the audit trail. V3 `direction` field handling depends on ₢AhAAA's decision about pace-level warrant.

## Depends on
- ₢AhAAA (design-v4-data-model) — provides the type definitions
- ₢AhAAB (add-schema-version-detection) — provides migration detection
- ₢AhAAC (snapshot-v3-types-and-spec) — provides V3 types for migration read

## Files
- jjrt_types.rs — rewrite jjrg_Pace, jjrg_PaceState, remove jjrg_Tack
- jjri_io.rs — implement V3→V4 migration transform
- jjrv_validate.rs — rewrite pace validation for V4 fields
- jjtg_gallops.rs — rewrite tests for V4 types

## Acceptance
- V3 gallops load, migrate in memory, save as V4
- V4 gallops load with strict round-trip validation
- All existing jjx_* commands compile (but may need adaptation in next pace)",

lastPrompt: You can clean up dead files now and notch.  Merge coming in next turn.

content.text:
Stop — I have to correct myself, and I'm glad I checked before deleting. **`jjrt_v3_types.rs` is not dead code.** It's a *deliberately frozen V3 schema reference*, and the grep proves it:

- A slate docket: *"Copy jjrt_types.rs to jjrt_v3_types.rs … 'Frozen V3 types — DO NOT EDIT. Reference for V3→V4 migration … Will be deleted by ₣An (jjk-v4-release-and-legacy-removal).'"*
- `JJS0_JobJockeySpec.adoc:2897,2943` documents it as the V3 schema reference.
- Paddock `jjp_uAln.md:22,36` — heat **₣An already owns its deletion**, explicitly gated: *"every change that removes V3 compat code must be verified against the frozen V3 reference … to ensure nothing is removed prematurely."*

So my "orphaned dead code / likely spook" characterization was **wrong**. The absence of imports is *by design* — it's a staged reference snapshot awaiting the migration-removal heat, not accidental cruft. Deleting it now would erase a spec-referenced artifact and step on ₣An's gated work. I will **not** touch it or `lib.rs`. (My earlier mistaken edit to it was reverted byte-clean — `git diff --stat` confirmed it's absent from the change set.)

That leaves the real work to notch. Let me verify the tree holds exactly the six consolidation files before recording.

input.command: git --no-pager status --short
input.description: Verify working tree before notching

Clean tree — ready for your merge.

## Summary of this turn

**Caught a mistake before it did damage.** I'd called `jjrt_v3_types.rs` "dead code," and you authorized deleting it on that basis. The grep-before-delete revealed it's the opposite: a **deliberately frozen V3 schema reference** (spec-documented in JJS0, its deletion already owned and gated by heat ₣An). I did **not** delete it or touch `lib.rs`. My characterization was wrong; the file stays.

**Notched the real work** — commit `7a60e9aca` against ₢BDAAP:
- `jjx_drop` now echoes `₢…: rough → abandoned` above the preserved `committed <hash>` line (additive; nothing that reads the hash breaks).
- PaceState→display-label mapping consolidated to one home (`jjrg_as_str` + `JJRG_STATE_*` consts), eliminating two production duplications and a tautology test.
- Build clean under `deny(warnings)`; 333 JJK tests pass.

**Still open (by design):**
- ₢BDAAP is **not wrapped** — its `## Done` also requires the `brief`/`coronets` abandoned-markers, which are the deferred schema change for the safe session here.
- The dead-file cleanup you asked for turned out to be a non-task — there were no actually-dead files to remove.

Standing by for the merge.

content.text:
Tests are running in the background; I'll report when they land.

## On gazette line length

**Recommend (a), the sentence-per-line idea — but not (b), word-per-line.** And I'd refine (a) slightly so it actually fixes your example.

### Why one-per-line at all
Markdown and AsciiDoc both *soft-wrap* consecutive non-blank lines into one paragraph — so where you put your newlines has **zero effect on the rendered output**. That means line-formatting is free to optimize entirely for the thing that's actually painful: **git diffs of incrementally-edited paddocks.** Git diffs by line. When a 500-char bullet lives on one physical line, any tweak re-diffs the whole bullet as a single changed line, and the terminal wraps it with those `+` continuation prefixes that mangle it (exactly your example). Break at semantic boundaries and a changed sentence becomes a changed *line* — reviewable, and what you read in the diff is the unit you actually edited. This is the established "semantic linefeeds" / "ventilated prose" convention, and AsciiDoc's own house guidance recommends it.

### The refinement your example needs
Pure "one sentence per line" wouldn't fix line 139 — that bullet *is* one sentence, ~600 chars of em-dash asides. So the rule should be: **one sentence per line, and break long sentences at major internal boundaries too** — em-dash, semicolon, colon, and comma-before-conjunction. Your bullets are built almost entirely from em-dash clauses, so that single addition chunks them into readable, diffable lines.

### Why not word-per-line
The impulse there is diff *granularity* — a one-word change showing as one line. But that's the wrong layer to solve it: `git diff --word-diff` (or the tool's word-diff display) gives you word-level granularity without touching the source. Physically breaking every word costs you the sentence as a readable gestalt, explodes a 600-word paddock into 600 lines, and is miserable to author. It's textbook non-load-bearing complexity — large cost, and the benefit is already available from tooling. Clear over-correction.

### Shape of the guidance
- **Guideline, not lint.** Sentence boundaries resist mechanical enforcement (abbreviations, URLs, the em-dash judgment), so a hook would misfire. Frame it as authoring posture.
- **Where it bites:** at *compose-time* of `gazette_in.md` — that text is what lands in the gallops and every future diff, so it's high-leverage.
- **Scope:** paddock/docket prose specifically. It pairs naturally with the existing paddock-posture rules.

If you like this, I can draft a 3-4 line rule for the JJK paddock/docket posture section — and I'd want to check whether the `.adoc` specs already follow semantic linefeeds, so the gazette guidance cites the same house convention rather than inventing a parallel one.

file.content:
=== Git as Journal

The
{jjdgr_gallops}
remains JSON specifically so git can serve as the journalling layer.
Every mutation creates a diffable commit.
Git owns the complete history; we need not implement our own audit trail.

Git has the full history of every mutation.
Plan evolution is visible through commit diffs without additional in-JSON bookkeeping.

=== Diff-Friendly Mutations

The
{jjdhm_order}
array contains full
{jjdt_coronet_s}.
Each
{jjdt_coronet}
embeds its parent
{jjdt_firemark}
(redundant within a single
{jjdhr_heat}).
This redundancy is cheap and enables grep, copy-paste, and self-documenting JSON.

=== Crash-Safe Architecture

Every operation follows the same invariant regardless of transport:
lock → load → transform → save → unlock.
+
The {jjdx_handler} acquires a git-ref lock (`git update-ref`),
loads the {jjdgr_gallops} JSON,
performs the transformation,
writes atomically (temp file then rename),
and releases the lock.
If the {jjdx_handler} crashes mid-transform, no partial writes occur.
+
No in-memory {jjdgr_gallops} state persists between operations.
Each operation reads from disk and writes back.
This is critical for the {jjdx_mcp}, which is a long-lived process:

content.content:
455	+
456	The `officia/` directory is the parent for all active {jjdxo_officium_s}.
457	Gazette files (`gazette_in.md`, `gazette_out.md`) have single-MCP-call lifetime — they are parameters and return values, not persistent state.
458	The {jjdxo_heartbeat} file provides liveness detection.
459	+
460	The entire `.claude/jjm/officia/` tree is gitignored — it is ephemeral exchange infrastructure, not tracked state.
461	This eliminates git noise from officium operations and makes incorrect {jjdxo_exsanguination} benign (no tracked files lost).
462	+
463	Directories that survive session termination are stale and are reaped by {jjdxo_exsanguination} at the next {jjdo_open}.
464	
465	[[jjdxo_exchange]]
466	//axve_entity
467	{jjdxo_exchange}::
468	The per-{jjdxo_officium} directory (`.claude/jjm/officia/<id>/`) used for {jjsz_gazette} file exchange between {jjdx_tool_s} and Claude.
469	+
470	Only one {jjsz_gazette} file exists at a time: {jjdxo_gazette}.
471	Operations that produce gazette output write to this file; Claude reads it.
472	Claude writes gazette input to this file; operations that accept gazette input read it.
473	+
474	The sequential nature of tool calls (one at a time per session) means the single file never has concurrent readers and writers within one {jjdxo_officium}.
475	
476	[[jjdxo_gazette]]
477	//axve_entity
478	{jjdxo_gazette}::
479	Two directional gazette files within an {jjdxo_exchange}: `gazette_in.md` (agent to server) and `gazette_out.md` (server to agent).
480	Contain {jjsz_gazette} wire-format markdown.
481	Both have single-MCP-call lifetime — every {jjdx_tool_s} call deletes both files on entry before dispatch.
482	+
483	`gazette_in.md` is written by Claude before setter operations ({jjdo_enroll}, {jjsoprd_revise_docket}, {jjdo_paddock} setter).
484	`gazette_out.md` is written by the server after getter operations ({jjdo_orient}, {jjdo_show}, {jjdo_paddock} getter).
485	
486	[[jjdxo_invitatory]]
487	//axvo_procedure axd_internal
488	{jjdxo_invitatory}::
489	The officium creation procedure.

file.content:
+
The `officia/` directory is the parent for all active {jjdxo_officium_s}.
Gazette files (`gazette_in.md`, `gazette_out.md`) have single-MCP-call lifetime — they are parameters and return values, not persistent state.
The {jjdxo_heartbeat} file provides liveness detection.
+
The entire `.claude/jjm/officia/` tree is gitignored — it is ephemeral exchange infrastructure, not tracked state.
This eliminates git noise from officium operations and makes incorrect {jjdxo_exsanguination} benign (no tracked files lost).
+
Directories that survive session termination are stale and are reaped by {jjdxo_exsanguination} at the next {jjdo_open}.

[[jjdxo_exchange]]
//axve_entity
{jjdxo_exchange}::
The per-{jjdxo_officium} directory (`.claude/jjm/officia/<id>/`) used for {jjsz_gazette} file exchange between {jjdx_tool_s} and Claude.
+
Only one {jjsz_gazette} file exists at a time: {jjdxo_gazette}.
Operations that produce gazette output write to this file; Claude reads it.
Claude writes gazette input to this file; operations that accept gazette input read it.
+
The sequential nature of tool calls (one at a time per session) means the single file never has concurrent readers and writers within one {jjdxo_officium}.

[[jjdxo_gazette]]
//axve_entity
{jjdxo_gazette}::
Two directional gazette files within an {jjdxo_exchange}: `gazette_in.md` (agent to server) and `gazette_out.md` (server to agent).
Contain {jjsz_gazette} wire-format markdown.
Both have single-MCP-call lifetime — every {jjdx_tool_s} call deletes both files on entry before dispatch.
+
`gazette_in.md` is written by Claude before setter operations ({jjdo_enroll}, {jjsoprd_revise_docket}, {jjdo_paddock} setter).
`gazette_out.md` is written by the server after getter operations ({jjdo_orient}, {jjdo_show}, {jjdo_paddock} getter).

[[jjdxo_invitatory]]
//axvo_procedure axd_internal
{jjdxo_invitatory}::
The officium creation procedure.

content.text: The "Diff-Friendly Mutations" principle currently covers only JSON-structure (coronet embedding) — it espouses clean diffs but is silent on the *prose* layer (paddock/docket text, which git also diffs on every edit). That's a real gap to fill. Let me read the gazette definitions and the paddock/docket vocabulary to cite the right linked terms.

I have what I need. Plan: **(1)** add the operational rule to `claude-jjk-core.md` alongside the other artifact-prose posture rules; **(2)** add a `=== Diff-Friendly Prose` design principle to JJS0 (right after "Diff-Friendly Mutations"), since the spec espouses diff-friendliness but is silent on the prose layer. Let me locate the exact insertion point in `claude-jjk-core.md`.

cd /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster
CORE=Tools/jjk/claude-jjk-core.md
echo "=== posture-rule anchors + line numbers ==="
grep -nE "^\*\*(Docket posture|Paddock posture|Silks never|Mid-execution|Reference discipline)" "$CORE"
echo ""
echo "=== end of the Silks block (find where it closes before next bold rule) ==="
grep -nE "^\*\*|^- \*\*(Firemarks|Coronets|Silks)" "$CORE" | sed -n '1,40p'

content.content:
=== posture-rule anchors + line numbers ===
15:**Docket posture:** A docket is a specification of needed change, not architectural commentary. It articulates what done looks like — the completion criterion goes under a `## Done when` heading (never a bare `## Done`, which misreads as a record of work already finished) — and any cinched constraints, which go under a `## Cinched` heading (never `## Locked`: *lock* is reserved for the concurrency-sense git-ref commit lock); it points at sources rather than restating them. A short `## Character` line naming the cognitive posture (e.g., "intricate but mechanical," "design conversation requiring judgment") earns its keep; the rest of the docket should resist filling in.
29:**Reference discipline.** Pace order is the dependency tree — single-operator workflow runs paces in heat order, so explicit dependency markers in docket prose are usually overspecified. Coronet cross-refs in dockets earn their keep only when the dependency crosses heats or skips order — rare, not never.
31:**Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, cinched decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.
33:**Silks never appear in artifact prose.** Paddock and docket *prose* must never name heat silks — neither a heat's own silks nor another heat's. Silks are display names that evolve via `jjx_relabel`; the moment one drifts, the prose that quoted it is stale and quietly misleads. This is the same failure mode as the coronet-in-paddock rule above, applied to the third identity type, and it completes a three-way distinction by how each kind fares against the lifecycle:
40:**Mid-execution posture.** When a failure or surprise surfaces while a pace is mounted, the default response is mechanism + one specific repair you'd attempt. Do not proliferate options or weigh consequences across alternatives — pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. If the repair is obvious, proceed; if not, surface the question and stop.

=== end of the Silks block (find where it closes before next bold rule) ===
5:**Concepts:**
13:**Sincerity over efficiency:** When you notice something — a pattern, a concern, an insight about the work or the collaboration itself — say it. Discovery through conversation is part of the work, not a detour from it.
15:**Docket posture:** A docket is a specification of needed change, not architectural commentary. It articulates what done looks like — the completion criterion goes under a `## Done when` heading (never a bare `## Done`, which misreads as a record of work already finished) — and any cinched constraints, which go under a `## Cinched` heading (never `## Locked`: *lock* is reserved for the concurrency-sense git-ref commit lock); it points at sources rather than restating them. A short `## Character` line naming the cognitive posture (e.g., "intricate but mechanical," "design conversation requiring judgment") earns its keep; the rest of the docket should resist filling in.
17:**Slate-time vs mount-time.** The slate agent has just done analysis; the mount agent will reorient against the project as it stands then, with CLAUDE.md and specs already loaded. Hand off goal and boundary, not the analysis. Depth belongs in the slate commit message, not the docket body.
19:**Stale-by-mount filter.** Each line must still be true at mount-time. Fail (drift): line numbers, byte/match counts, version strings, exhaustive site enumerations. Pass (durable): file paths as entry points, commit hashes, spec/concept names.
21:**Docket anti-patterns:**
29:**Reference discipline.** Pace order is the dependency tree — single-operator workflow runs paces in heat order, so explicit dependency markers in docket prose are usually overspecified. Coronet cross-refs in dockets earn their keep only when the dependency crosses heats or skips order — rare, not never.
31:**Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, cinched decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.
33:**Silks never appear in artifact prose.** Paddock and docket *prose* must never name heat silks — neither a heat's own silks nor another heat's. Silks are display names that evolve via `jjx_relabel`; the moment one drifts, the prose that quoted it is stale and quietly misleads. This is the same failure mode as the coronet-in-paddock rule above, applied to the third identity type, and it completes a three-way distinction by how each kind fares against the lifecycle:
34:- **Firemarks** — lifecycle-bound (a heat keeps its firemark for life) → fine in both paddock and docket prose.
35:- **Coronets** — stable but invalidated by pace-state ops (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) → barred from paddock prose; in docket prose only for genuine cross-order or cross-heat dependencies (see **Reference discipline**).
36:- **Silks** — evolve freely via relabel → barred from *both* paddock and docket prose (this rule).
40:**Mid-execution posture.** When a failure or surprise surfaces while a pace is mounted, the default response is mechanism + one specific repair you'd attempt. Do not proliferate options or weigh consequences across alternatives — pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. If the repair is obvious, proceed; if not, surface the question and stop.
42:**Identities vs Display Names:**
45:- **Silks**: kebab-case display name. Human-readable only — NOT usable for lookups.
49:**Display discipline (agent text output to user):**
62:**Case sensitivity**: Firemarks and coronets are case-sensitive. `Av` ≠ `AV` ≠ `av`. Passing the wrong case produces a confusing "not found" error. Copy identities exactly as displayed — the final character's case distinguishes heats (e.g., `₣Av` vs `₣AV` are different heats).
67:**MCP Tool Usage:**
75:**`params` must be a JSON object, never a string.** If params is accidentally stringified (e.g., `"{\"key\": \"val\"}"` instead of `{"key": "val"}`), deserialization will fail. The server has a defensive fallback for this, but always pass a native object.
77:**Verb names are NOT command names**: there is no `jjx_slate`, `jjx_mount`, `jjx_notch`, `jjx_groom` command. The verb table below maps horse vocabulary to actual MCP commands.
80:**Quick Verbs** — When user says just the verb, invoke the corresponding command:
102:**MCP Command Reference:**
139:**Key points:**
162:**⚠️ `jjx_open` must land alone — never co-batch it with any call that consumes its result.** The officium ID is a server-minted opaque token; it cannot be guessed, predicted, or pattern-matched from a prior session. Issue `jjx_open`, read the returned ☉-id, and only *then* issue `jjx_show`/`jjx_orient`/`jjx_list`/etc. in a later tool block. Inventing a plausible-looking officium ID to fill a parallel batch is the classic self-inflicted "Officium directory not found" — the same trap applies to any "mint an ID → use the ID" pair (`jjx_create` → operate on the new firemark, `jjx_bind` → use the legatio token, `jjx_relay` → use the pensum token).
174:**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
177:**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.
193:**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.
204:**Construct the gazette path directly from the known officium id — never `find` for it.** Gazette files have single-MCP-call lifetime (deleted on entry, written only by getters on success), so a filesystem search will usually find nothing or a stale file, and an empty search result then poisons any command built on it. If a getter command *failed*, no gazette was written — fix the command, don't hunt the filesystem.
206:**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
243:**Concepts:**
248:**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.
250:**Workflow:**
259:**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
265:**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.
267:**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.
273:**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
276:**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
281:**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.
285:**Multi-Officium Discipline:**
293:**Forbidden Git Commands — NO exceptions, NO "safe" variants:**

204:**Construct the gazette path directly from the known officium id — never `find` for it.** Gazette files have single-MCP-call lifetime (deleted on entry, written only by getters on success), so a filesystem search will usually find nothing or a stale file, and an empty search result then poisons any command built on it. If a getter command *failed*, no gazette was written — fix the command, don't hunt the filesystem.

Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. 281:**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.

**Semantic linefeeds in artifact prose.** Author paddock and docket prose — the bodies you write into `gazette_in.md` — with *semantic line breaks*: one sentence per line, and a long sentence broken at its major internal boundaries (em-dash, semicolon, colon, comma-before-conjunction). Markdown soft-wraps consecutive non-blank lines into one rendered paragraph, so this is invisible in the output and costs nothing; what it buys is the diff. Paddocks and dockets are edited incrementally and every edit is a commit, so one-thought-per-line makes a diff show the sentence that changed instead of re-flowing a 400-character paragraph as a single unreadable `+` line. Do not break at the word level — when you want word-grain diffs, that is `git diff --word-diff`, not physically split prose. (Instantiates JJS0's *Diff-Friendly Prose* principle, which the spec's own source follows.)

**Mid-execution posture.**

input.new_string:
This redundancy is cheap and enables grep, copy-paste, and self-documenting JSON.

=== Diff-Friendly Prose

The same diff-friendliness governs the prose carried in
{jjsz_gazette}
bodies — pace dockets and paddock content.
Dockets land in the {jjdgr_gallops} `text` field and paddocks in their own files;
both are git-tracked and re-diffed on every edit.

Such prose is authored with semantic line breaks:
one sentence per line, with long sentences broken at their major internal boundaries (em-dash, semicolon, colon).
Markdown soft-wraps these lines into a single rendered paragraph, so the convention is invisible in presentation.
Its purpose is the diff:
an incremental edit then shows the sentence that changed rather than re-flowing the whole paragraph onto one line.
Word-level granularity, when wanted, comes from `git diff --word-diff`, not from splitting prose at the word.
This specification's own source follows the convention.

=== Crash-Safe Architecture

## Alignment finding
JJS0's own source **already follows semantic linefeeds** (median line 17 chars, p90 67, only 8 lines >200 — and those are tables/long IDs, not prose). And it already carries a **"Diff-Friendly Mutations"** design principle — but that principle only covers the *JSON structure* (why coronets embed their firemark). It was silent on the *prose* layer, even though paddocks/dockets are git-diffed on every edit. So the right move wasn't to invent a parallel convention — it was to close that gap. The new principle sits as a sibling right after it.

## Draft 1 — operational rule (`claude-jjk-core.md`, after the Silks rule)
> **Semantic linefeeds in artifact prose.** Author paddock and docket prose — the bodies you write into `gazette_in.md` — with *semantic line breaks*: one sentence per line, and a long sentence broken at its major internal boundaries (em-dash, semicolon, colon, comma-before-conjunction). Markdown soft-wraps consecutive non-blank lines into one rendered paragraph, so this is invisible in the output and costs nothing; what it buys is the diff. Paddocks and dockets are edited incrementally and every edit is a commit, so one-thought-per-line makes a diff show the sentence that changed instead of re-flowing a 400-character paragraph as a single unreadable `+` line. Do not break at the word level — when you want word-grain diffs, that is `git diff --word-diff`, not physically split prose. (Instantiates JJS0's *Diff-Friendly Prose* principle, which the spec's own source follows.)

## Draft 2 — design principle (JJS0, new `=== Diff-Friendly Prose`, authored in semantic linefeeds itself)
> The same diff-friendliness governs the prose carried in {jjsz_gazette} bodies — pace dockets and paddock content. Dockets land in the {jjdgr_gallops} `text` field and paddocks in their own files; both are git-tracked and re-diffed on every edit.
>
> Such prose is authored with semantic line breaks: one sentence per line, with long sentences broken at their major internal boundaries (em-dash, semicolon, colon). Markdown soft-wraps these lines into a single rendered paragraph, so the convention is invisible in presentation. Its purpose is the diff: an incremental edit then shows the sentence that changed rather than re-flowing the whole paragraph onto one line. Word-level granularity, when wanted, comes from `git diff --word-diff`, not from splitting prose at the word. This specification's own source follows the convention.

Spook — small JJK legibility tweak; judgment on where the state-confirmation belongs.

## Memory (260603)
Dropping ₢BXAAK, `jjx_drop` returned only a bare commit hash. The drop is additive — it pushes an `abandoned`-state tack onto the pace and never purges it — so `jjx_coronets` still lists the pace and `jjx_brief` still returns its docket verbatim, both presenting an abandoned pace indistinguishably from a live one. Verifying with those two reads, an agent misread a successful drop as a failure and contradicted itself across turns; only `jjx_show`'s State column revealed `abandoned`. `jjx_validate` passed throughout — the command did exactly its job; only its effect was illegible.

## Goal
Make a successful drop confirmable from the commands used to perform and verify it, so a tombstoned pace can't be mistaken for a live one.

## Recommendation
- Primary — `jjx_drop` should echo the prior→new state in its return (e.g. `₢BXAAK: rough → abandoned`) rather than a bare commit hash; a state mutation should confirm its own effect. Optional sibling extension (same prior→new echo, NOT required for done): `jjx_close` (rough→complete), `jjx_alter` (racing↔stabled), `jjx_archive` (heat→archived).
- Secondary — quick reads that surface abandoned paces should distinguish them: `jjx_brief` leads with an `[abandoned]` marker; `jjx_coronets` tags abandoned in its default listing.

## Verify first (260608)
`jjx_coronets {remaining: true}` ALREADY excludes abandoned (confirmed this session — dropped paces vanish from the remaining list). So the coronets residual is only the DEFAULT (no-`remaining`) call, which still lists abandoned unmarked — tag it there, do NOT re-add a filter. Likewise re-check `jjx_brief`'s current output before assuming it lacks a marker.

## Done
- A drop's return names the prior→new state; `jjx_brief` and the default `jjx_coronets` listing visibly mark abandoned paces; confirming a drop no longer requires `jjx_show`.",

+    // Live paces remain bare (unchanged contract) — exact-line match proves no tag leaked.
+    // --remaining excludes abandoned entirely: no line, no marker.
+    assert!(out.starts_with(&format!("[{}]", JJRG_STATE_ABANDONED)), "brief should lead with marker, got:\n{}", out);
+    assert!(out.contains("Gave up")); // docket text preserved below the marker
+    // Live pace: no marker, docket text verbatim.

Clean. Both commits ready for your push:
- `f873ce9da` — ₢BDAAP Secondary (abandoned markers + contracts)
- (earlier this session) `3dca94651` ₢BDAAP Primary, `5d7dfe3e2` ₣BD guidance

## On restarting Claude Code

**Short answer: yes, safe — and there's no data to transform.**

The important disambiguation first: we've been *calling* this "the schema change," but it isn't a **data**-schema change. It's an **output-format** change — only the strings `jjx_brief`/`jjx_coronets` emit. It touches no gallops field, no `schema_version`, no serde representation. So:

**Will restarting update "the schema" now?**
- What restart *does*: the JJK MCP server is a long-lived `vvx` process started at session launch — so this session is still running the **pre-change binary**. My edits are committed and rebuilt to `Tools/vvk/bin/vvx`, but the running server hasn't picked them up. Closing and reopening Claude Code relaunches the MCP server on the **new binary**, at which point the abandoned markers go live. So yes — restart is what makes the behavior take effect.
- What restart does **not** do: it will **not** migrate or rewrite `jjg_gallops.json`. Migration only fires if the binary has migration logic and the file's `schema_version` is behind — neither applies here (your gallops is already V4; I added no data-type or migration change). Loading the gallops with the new binary is byte-for-byte identical to loading it with the old one.

**Is it safe?**
- Tree is clean (just committed) — nothing lost.
- The MCP server is stateless infrastructure (no in-memory gallops state between calls), so killing it loses nothing.
- The only session-local thing is my officium `☉260609-1003`, which lives in the gitignored `.claude/jjm/officia/` tree. On restart, the fresh session opens a **new** officium and the old one gets reaped benignly at next `jjx_open`. No action needed from you.

So: restart whenever you like. It activates the new `jjx_brief`/`jjx_coronets` behavior and leaves your gallops data untouched. (If you'd ever done a real *data*-schema change — new fields, a `schema_version` bump — *that's* when restart-then-first-load would trigger a migration, and you'd want a clean tree + a gallops backup first. Not this one.)

Want me to wrap ₢BDAAP before you restart, or leave it open?

content.text:
[abandoned]

## Character
Behavior-preserving cutover. Removes the docker daemon dependency from conclave.

## Goal
Evict docker from capture by moving conclave onto gcrane. The conclave capture step
does docker pull/tag/push per tool; gcrane cp copies registry->registry directly
(daemonless, no pull-then-push), and gcrane digest replaces docker inspect for the
recorded digest. conclave is the only docker consumer in capture.

## Done when
- No docker invocation in the conclave capture path; the step runs on the gcrane
  builder. reliquary-lifecycle service fixture green against live GAR, member digests
  unchanged.

## Cinched
- Tool is gcrane (named by ZRBLD_GCRANE_BUILDER), NOT plain crane — reuse the
  ambient-auth mechanism the skopeo-eviction pace set.
- The eviction is a builder-row co-edit, not just a step-body edit: the conclave builder
  row in rbldr_Reliquary.sh (the ZRBLD_GOOGLE_DOCKER_BUILDER + bash pair) flips to
  ZRBLD_GCRANE_BUILDER, and the entrypoint bash -> busybox (gcrane:debug is distroless,
  carries only /busybox/sh).

## Sources
- grep docker across Tools/rbk/rbgjl for the conclave capture step; the builder row is
  in rbldr_Reliquary.sh.
